notes: Internal methodology source card for validating the source registry path.
title: Lumenary Original Idea Methodology

notes: Primary Upanishadic source for the unseen seer/hearer/knower and inner ruler language. High priority for Lumenary's residue-policy and remainder-pressure thread.
title: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 3.7.23

notes: Primary source for the identity formula 'tat tvam asi'. Useful for testing whether identity claims function as metaphysical assertion, instruction, or recognition formula.
title: Chandogya Upanishad 6.8.7 Tat Tvam Asi

notes: Primary source for waking, dreaming, deep sleep, and turiya. Useful for comparing state models of consciousness with witness and self-negation claims.
title: Mandukya Upanishad

notes: Advaita teaching text attributed to Shankara. Useful for comparing later Advaita instruction with earlier Upanishadic language and for checking whether de-objectification becomes explicit witness ontology.
title: Shankara Upadesa Sahasri

notes: Primary early Buddhist source for the not-self analysis of the five aggregates. Central counterpoint to Upanishadic residue and witness language.
title: SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta

notes: Primary source using foam, bubble, mirage, and illusion similes for the aggregates. Useful for testing whether consciousness is treated as insubstantial rather than as final witness.
title: SN 22.95 Phenapindupama Sutta

notes: Primary source on the root of conceptual proliferation and the way different persons conceive experience. Useful for Lumenary's inference-policy and reification analysis.
title: MN 1 Mulapariyaya Sutta

notes: Ineffability, wu as generative non-being, cosmogonic sequence, wu wei, return.
title: Dao De Jing

notes: Focused Dao De Jing source for learning, diminishing, and wu wei. Useful for comparing negation-by-reduction with self-negation and de-identification practices.
title: Dao De Jing Chapter 48

notes: Primary Daoist source for wandering, perspective shifting, fasting the heart-mind, and self-forgetting. Useful for a non-ontological model of spiritual transformation.
title: Zhuangzi

notes: Primary Neoplatonic source for the One, Intellect, Soul, ascent, and self-transcendence. Useful for comparing unity claims with Advaita and Sufi metaphysics.
title: Plotinus Enneads

notes: Focused Plotinus source for Intellect and the One. Useful for testing whether unity is framed as ontological ground, contemplative ascent, or noetic identity.
title: Plotinus Enneads Fifth Ennead

notes: Scholarly overview of Plotinus and the three principles: the One, Intellect, and Soul. Use as secondary guardrail when interpreting Plotinus primary texts.
title: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy Plotinus

notes: Sufi ethical and contemplative source centered on knowledge of self, God, world, afterlife, recollection, and love. Useful for comparing self-knowledge and God-knowledge without collapsing them into nondual identity.
title: Al-Ghazali The Alchemy of Happiness

notes: Abridged public-domain source related to Conference of the Birds. Useful for studying annihilation, journey structure, and allegorical self-overcoming.
title: Attar Bird Parliament

notes: Mystical odes with authorial commentary. Useful for distinguishing symbolic love language, metaphysical interpretation, and risks of over-literalizing Sufi unity claims.
title: Ibn al-Arabi Tarjuman al-Ashwaq

notes: Foundational IIT paper proposing consciousness as integrated information. Use only as empirical-adjacent/scientific comparison; do not equate it with spiritual claims about awareness.
title: Tononi Information Integration Theory of Consciousness

notes: Scientific source on global neuronal workspace and broadcasting. Useful for distinguishing access-consciousness from metaphysical witness-consciousness.
title: Global Neuronal Workspace as a Broadcasting Network

notes: Scientific model connecting interoceptive prediction, agency, and presence. Useful for Lumenary's remainder-pressure idea as empirical-adjacent grounding.
title: An Interoceptive Predictive Coding Model of Conscious Presence

notes: Review-oriented scientific/philosophical source on selfhood and consciousness. Useful for separating narrative self, minimal self, ownership, and witness-like phenomenology.
title: I and Me The Self in the Context of Consciousness

notes: Official CERN overview of the Standard Model. Use as a baseline map for matter particles, force carriers, the Higgs sector, and the fact that gravity is not included in the Standard Model.
title: CERN The Standard Model

notes: Current Particle Data Group reference for particle properties, constants, Standard Model reviews, and experimental constraints. Use as the correction layer when a speculative idea depends on real particle-physics values.
title: Particle Data Group Review of Particle Physics

notes: Official CERN explanation of Higgs-field mass generation and particles as field excitations. Use to discipline claims about matter as field-pattern rather than substance, while avoiding metaphysical overreach.
title: CERN What Is Special About The Higgs Boson

notes: Primary ATLAS discovery paper for a new particle compatible with the Standard Model Higgs boson. Useful as a hard empirical anchor for Higgs-field and mass-generation discussions.
title: ATLAS Higgs Boson Discovery Paper

notes: Primary CMS discovery paper for a boson near 125 GeV. Pair with the ATLAS paper before treating Higgs-related mass claims as experimentally grounded.
title: CMS Higgs Boson Discovery Paper

notes: Foundational electroweak unification paper. Use as formal-theory grounding for symmetry breaking, gauge structure, and mass terms before drawing analogies to spiritual unity or differentiation.
title: Weinberg A Model of Leptons

notes: Department of Energy overview of the strong interaction. Use as a guardrail against the common error that the Higgs field explains all ordinary matter mass; hadronic mass requires QCD and confinement.
title: DOE Explains The Strong Force

notes: Official CERN source on quark-gluon plasma and matter under extreme energy density. Useful for grounding claims about matter phase, confinement, deconfinement, and the early-universe material state.
title: CERN Heavy Ions and Quark Gluon Plasma

notes: Original Noether theorem source. Use to ground the relationship between time-translation symmetry and conservation laws before making philosophical claims about time, order, and invariance.
title: Noether Invariant Variation Problems

notes: Primary special-relativity source for simultaneity, clock synchronization, and the replacement of universal time with observer-relative temporal coordinates.
title: Einstein On The Electrodynamics Of Moving Bodies

notes: Primary source for the spacetime formulation of special relativity. Use as the anchor when distinguishing experienced time, coordinate time, and four-dimensional spacetime structure.
title: Minkowski Space And Time

notes: Primary high-energy-physics paper reporting direct time-reversal violation in the neutral B meson system. Use for physical time-asymmetry claims, not for broad claims about psychological or cosmic time.
title: BABAR Observation Of Time Reversal Violation

notes: Review of the problem of time in quantum gravity. Use as a map of competing strategies: time before quantization, emergent time, timeless records, and relational observables.
title: Anderson The Problem Of Time In Quantum Gravity

notes: Formal source for partial and complete observables. Useful for Lumenary's inference-policy work because it treats observability as relational without turning relation into mystical identity.
title: Rovelli Partial Observables

notes: Source for the idea that apparent dynamics can be represented through correlations with internal clock readings inside a stationary global description. Useful for emergent-time hypotheses.
title: Page And Wootters Evolution Without Evolution

notes: Canonical quantum-gravity source associated with the Wheeler-DeWitt equation. Use as a historical and formal anchor for timeless wavefunction discussions, with caution about unresolved interpretation.
title: DeWitt Quantum Theory Of Gravity The Canonical Theory

notes: Foundational source for the conditions required to generate cosmic matter-antimatter asymmetry. Use to ground claims about why the universe contains matter rather than equal annihilating matter and antimatter.
title: Sakharov CP Violation And Baryon Asymmetry

notes: Official CERN overview of antimatter, antimatter experiments, and the unresolved matter-antimatter asymmetry problem. Use as a current experimental-context card.
title: CERN Antimatter

notes: Planck Collaboration cosmological-parameter paper. Use as a quantitative anchor for baryon density, dark matter density, and the matter content of the observable universe.
title: Planck 2018 Cosmological Parameters

notes: Official CERN overview of particle physics as the study of fundamental constituents of matter, plus adjacent CERN research areas. Use as a broad orientation source for the HEP corpus.
title: CERN Physics Programme

notes: Claude source card on Mandukya Upanishad with Gaudapada's Karika, focused on turiya, ajativada, waking/dream comparison, and Buddhist-Advaita translation strain.
title: Mandukya Upanishad with Gaudapada's Karika

notes: Drig-drishya-viveka, pancha kosha model, self-luminous witness after five-sheath negation.
title: Vivekachudamani

notes: Perspectival relativity, butterfly dream, xinzhai, zuowang, qi transformation.
title: Zhuangzi Inner Chapters

notes: Mone-proodos-epistrophe triad, henads, fractal emanation, participation.
title: Proclus Elements of Theology

notes: Turiya, ajativada, waking=dream. Bridge text between Hindu and Buddhist non-dual philosophy.
title: Mandukya Upanishad with Gaudapada Karika

notes: Radical idealism, nested dream cosmology, three akashas. Strongest pre-modern simulation hypothesis text.
title: Yoga Vasistha

notes: Sunyata=dependent origination (24:18), catuskoti, non-origination (1:1), self as catuskoti (18:6).
title: Nagarjuna MMK

notes: Citta-matra, alaya-vijnana, paravritti, ocean-waves metaphor. Bridge between Yogacara and Zen.
title: Lankavatara Sutra

notes: Being-time identity, nikon (absolute present), dharma-position. Parallels block universe in physics.
title: Dogen Shobogenzo Uji

notes: Form=emptiness, systematic negation of all Buddhist categories, two truths as co-extensive.
title: Heart Sutra

notes: Three hypostases, One beyond being/thought, epistrophe, henosis.
title: Plotinus Enneads V.1 and VI.9

notes: Phi as integrated information, constitutive panpsychism, 2025 Nature adversarial collaboration.
title: IIT Tononi

notes: Quantum gravity in microtubules, non-computable consciousness, Godelian argument.
title: Penrose Hameroff Orch-OR

notes: Copenhagen, Von Neumann-Wigner, many-worlds, QBism, decoherence. Observer role.
title: Measurement Problem in QM

notes: Markov blankets as self-boundary, precision relaxation meditation model, active inference.
title: Friston Free Energy Principle

notes: Fitness beats truth, desktop metaphor, conscious agents formalism. Parallels maya and citta-matra.
title: Hoffman Conscious Realism

notes: Information as fundamental substrate, delayed-choice experiment, participatory universe.
title: Wheeler It from Bit

notes: Wahdat al-wujud, tajalli (divine self-disclosure), barzakh, al-insan al-kamil, immutable entities.
title: Ibn Arabi Fusus al-Hikam

notes: Ecstatic self-identification with God. Fana, moth-flame allegory, Iblis dialogue. Parallels Aham Brahmasmi.
title: Al-Hallaj Ana al-Haqq and Kitab al-Tawasin

notes: Song of the Reed, love as primary epistemology, evolutionary consciousness, die before you die.
title: Rumi Masnavi Book 1

notes: Primary Christian source for Logos, light, life, incarnation, abiding, love, and relation to the Father. Useful for testing whether unity is presented as identity, communion, participation, or love.
title: Gospel of John

notes: Pauline source for love as the highest spiritual capacity and for the limits of knowledge without love. Useful for testing Lumenary's love-as-knowing thread against Christian practice and ethics.
title: 1 Corinthians 13

notes: Christian apophatic source for ascent by unknowing, negation, and divine darkness. Useful as a Western counterweight to Buddhist emptiness, Advaita neti neti, and Daoist namelessness.
title: Pseudo-Dionysius The Mystical Theology

notes: Christian inwardness source centered on memory, self-examination, desire, and God as more inward than the self. Useful for comparing interiority with witness, recollection, and the storage of identity in memory.
title: Augustine Confessions Book 10

notes: Early Christian monastic source for watchfulness, humility, silence, temptation, practice, and the transformation of attention in ordinary conduct. Useful for grounding method as lived discipline rather than doctrine alone.
title: Sayings of the Desert Fathers

notes: Hebrew Bible creation source for speech, ordering, light, image, breath, blessing, and Sabbath. Useful for comparing creation by word with Logos, emanation, and information-pattern models.
title: Genesis 1 Creation Word and Image

notes: Hebrew Bible source for the divine name, presence, calling, and revelation as encounter. Useful for testing whether ultimate reality is approached as being, relation, command, or liberating address.
title: Exodus 3 Divine Name

notes: Wisdom source for time, season, impermanence, limits of human control, and meaning under mortality. Useful for comparing lived time with physics time and contemplative acceptance.
title: Ecclesiastes 3 Time and Season

notes: Early Jewish mystical cosmology of letters, numbers, breath, and formation. Useful for comparing creation as linguistic structure with Logos, mantra, information, and formal pattern.
title: Sefer Yetzirah

notes: Kabbalistic source for Ein Sof, sefirot, emanation, symbolic reading, and divine-human participation. Useful for comparing Neoplatonic emanation, Sufi disclosure, and Christian participation without collapsing their differences.
title: Zohar

notes: Jewish philosophical source for negative theology and discipline around divine attributes. Useful as a rigorous guardrail for claims that name the absolute too quickly.
title: Maimonides Guide for the Perplexed Negative Theology

notes: Primary Zoroastrian liturgical and poetic source, including the Gathas. Useful for studying truth, choice, moral attention, good mind, cosmic order, and the way inner discernment becomes a world-shaping act.
title: Yasna and Gathas

notes: Zoroastrian source for law, purity, pollution, earth, death, and the struggle against destructive forces. Useful for testing how spiritual practice treats matter, contamination, order, and moral hygiene.
title: Vendidad

notes: Middle Persian Zoroastrian cosmology on creation, cosmic conflict, time, elements, matter, and final restoration. Useful for comparing sacred time, world repair, and matter as a contested field.
title: Bundahishn

notes: Zoroastrian encyclopedic source preserving doctrine, commentary, and older material. Useful as a guardrail for how later tradition organizes truth, memory, scripture, ethics, and cosmology.
title: Denkard

notes: Core Hermetic source for mind, cosmos, divine intellect, ascent, rebirth, and the relation between human knowing and cosmic order. Useful for comparing contemplative ascent with Neoplatonic, Christian, and Egyptian material.
title: Corpus Hermeticum

notes: Hermetic dialogue on divine presence, cosmos, sacred practice, image, and the animation of the world. Useful for testing claims about matter as dead substance versus matter as a participant in spirit.
title: Asclepius

notes: Nag Hammadi Hermetic ascent dialogue centered on instruction, vision, praise, and silence. Useful for studying how a method moves from teaching to direct seeing, then from seeing to speechless knowing.
title: Discourse on the Eighth and Ninth

notes: Early Egyptian mortuary and royal ascent texts. Useful for studying speech, transformation, star imagery, continuity after death, and the relation between ritual utterance and cosmic passage.
title: Pyramid Texts

notes: Egyptian funerary corpus on judgment, names, heart, transformation, and passage through danger. Useful for comparing ethical self-accounting, soul anatomy, and afterlife navigation with other traditions.
title: Egyptian Book of the Dead

notes: Egyptian theological source for creation through heart and tongue, with Ptah as ordering intelligence. Useful for comparing creation by speech with Genesis, Logos, mantra, and information-pattern models.
title: Shabaka Stone Memphite Theology

notes: Jain source for non-harm, restraint, ascetic discipline, attention to living beings, and the ethics of movement. Useful for testing whether liberation is framed as insight alone or as total conduct.
title: Acaranga Sutra

notes: Systematic Jain source for reality, soul, karma, influx, stoppage, shedding, and liberation. Useful for mapping how a tradition turns metaphysics into a precise practice of constraint.
title: Tattvartha Sutra

notes: Jain teaching source on discipline, death, renunciation, knowledge, and moral vigilance. Useful for comparing spiritual maturity as liberation from attachment with Buddhist, Hindu, and Christian monastic sources.
title: Uttaradhyayana Sutra

notes: Primary Sikh scripture centered on divine name, devotion, equality, remembrance, ego-loss, and life in the world. Useful for testing whether realization must leave ordinary life or transform it.
title: Guru Granth Sahib

notes: Foundational Sikh composition on one reality, command, truthful living, listening, remembrance, and humility. Useful for comparing direct practice with metaphysical claim and ethical return.
title: Japji Sahib

notes: Sikh source on bliss, song, true teaching, and the joy of alignment with divine command. Useful for comparing joy as a sign of integration rather than merely a pleasant state.
title: Anand Sahib

notes: Confucian source for moral learning, ritual, character, humane conduct, self-cultivation, and the teacher-student relation. Useful for grounding method as formation of personhood, not private experience alone.
title: Analects

notes: Confucian source for moral sprouts, cultivated goodness, political responsibility, and embodied ethical response. Useful for comparing innate moral capacity with enlightenment, grace, and awakened nature.
title: Mencius

notes: Confucian source on investigation, sincerity, ordering the self, family, state, and world. Useful for testing whether spiritual insight must scale outward into social order.
title: Great Learning

notes: Confucian source on equilibrium, harmony, sincerity, and the refined adjustment of conduct. Useful for comparing enlightenment as balance, attunement, and exact response rather than escape.
title: Doctrine of the Mean

notes: Pre-Socratic source for flux, logos, hidden harmony, fire, opposition, and the difficulty of waking perception. Useful for comparing change, order, and insight across Greek, Daoist, and physics sources.
title: Heraclitus Fragments

notes: Pre-Socratic source for being, non-being, truth, appearance, and the path of inquiry. Useful for testing claims about permanence, negation, and whether thought can reach what does not change.
title: Parmenides Fragments

notes: Platonic source on cosmos, soul, time, receptacle, mathematical order, and the relation between eternal model and visible world. Useful for comparing physics of time with ancient cosmology.
title: Plato Timaeus

notes: Greek hymn corpus tied to Orphic religious imagination. Useful for studying sacred address, purification, cosmic powers, soul descent, and the difference between philosophy and lived ritual.
title: Orphic Hymns

notes: Collection-level source for late antique Gnostic, Christian, Hermetic, and Platonic materials. Useful as a map of salvation by knowledge, hidden teaching, cosmic estrangement, and inner light.
title: Nag Hammadi Library

notes: Sayings source centered on hidden words, recognition, inward kingdom, and transformation of perception. Useful for comparing awakening as finding what is already present with Buddhist and Christian sources.
title: Gospel of Thomas

notes: Gnostic mythological source for emanation, error, world-making, the hidden divine, and rescue through revelation. Useful for testing when cosmology becomes a diagnosis of captivity.
title: Apocryphon of John

notes: Valentinian source on ignorance, error, rest, name, revelation, and return to the Father. Useful for comparing salvation as correction of mistaken perception with remembrance and awakening models.
title: Gospel of Truth

notes: Scholarly source collection for Manichaean teaching, practice, worship, and community texts. Useful for grounding claims about radical dualism, light, matter, mixture, discipline, and world-renunciation.
title: Manichaean Texts from the Roman Empire

notes: Bibliographic source record for a Coptic Manichaean hymn and prayer collection. Useful as a pointer for future close-reading of light, grief, liberation, and devotional dualism.
title: Manichaean Psalm Book

notes: Early Japanese source for kami, creation, descent, land, purity, and sacred genealogy. Useful for studying sacred presence in place, lineage, ritual order, and the animation of the world.
title: Kojiki

notes: Collection-level source for Shinto and early Japanese religious texts. Useful as a cautious entry point for kami, purity, ritual, place, and the coexistence of Shinto, Buddhist, and Confucian influence.
title: Shinto Documents

notes: Old Norse poetic source for gods, fate, creation, destruction, wisdom, ordeal, and world-tree cosmology. Useful for comparing fate, sacrifice, and cyclic renewal with other ancient sources.
title: Poetic Edda

notes: Norse visionary poem on creation, memory, gods, fate, catastrophe, and renewal. Useful for studying prophecy as world-memory and the way ending can function as disclosure.
title: Voluspa

notes: UNESCO reference for the Yoruba Ifa divination system as a living oral corpus of signs, verses, medicine, ethics, and guidance. Use cautiously: this is a living tradition reference, not permission to extract restricted practice.
title: Ifa Divination System

notes: Public educational source for the Kiche Maya Popol Vuh. Useful for studying creation, speech, darkness, dawn, failed formations, hero twins, underworld trial, and the making of human beings from maize.
title: Popol Vuh

notes: Bibliographic source record for a public edition of the Navajo creation story. Use cautiously and with respect for living-tradition boundaries: this should guide future practitioner-aware research, not extraction of restricted ceremonial knowledge.
title: Dine Bahane

notes: U.S. Surgeon General advisory on loneliness, isolation, social connection, belonging, health risk, and community repair. Use as a grounding source for teachings and practices aimed at disconnection, feeling unneeded, social withdrawal, and the loss of ordinary belonging.
title: Our Epidemic of Loneliness and Isolation

notes: World Health Organization report on mental health burden, social determinants, treatment gaps, stigma, and the need to transform care. Use as grounding for anxiety, depression, isolation, suffering, structural pressure, and teachings that must not pretend spiritual practice replaces clinical care.
title: World Mental Health Report

notes: SAMHSA national report on substance use, substance use disorder, mental illness, treatment, and co-occurring distress. Use as grounding for addiction, compulsion, relapse, withdrawal, shame, and the limits of reflective practices when clinical or peer support is needed.
title: 2023 National Survey on Drug Use and Health

notes: CDC public-health source on overdose, opioids, fentanyl, prevention, and harm reduction context. Use as a hard safety constraint: Lumenary practices must never replace emergency care, medication, treatment, or recovery support for addiction and withdrawal.
title: Drug Overdose Prevention

notes: U.S. Surgeon General advisory on social media and youth mental health. Use as grounding for attention fragmentation, comparison, loneliness, self-worth tied to visibility, and practices that help people notice how digital environments train desire and belonging.
title: Social Media and Youth Mental Health

notes: American Psychological Association report on stress, uncertainty, money, work, politics, safety, and the future. Use as grounding for modern overload, exhaustion, vigilance, helplessness, and teachings that must fit people living under real pressure.
title: Stress in America 2024

notes: World Health Organization definition of burn-out as a workplace phenomenon involving exhaustion, mental distance or cynicism, and reduced professional efficacy. Use as grounding for teachings and practices about work, effort, responsibility, recovery, and the danger of spiritualizing overwork.
title: Burn-out as an Occupational Phenomenon

notes: Pew Research Center study on where Americans say they find meaning, including family, career, money, spirituality, faith, friends, and health. Use as grounding for meaning loss, purpose, belonging, and how teachings should meet the actual places people already look for significance.
title: Where Americans Find Meaning in Life

notes: Curran and Hill meta-analysis on birth cohort changes in perfectionism from 1989 to 2016. Use as grounding for achievement pressure, socially prescribed perfectionism, performance-based self-worth, comparison, shame, and why result-release practices are targeted remedies rather than universal practices.
title: Perfectionism Is Increasing Over Time

notes: CDC source on suicide as a public-health problem, risk, prevention, and warning signs. Use as a safety constraint for teachings on despair, meaning, grief, disconnection, and burdensomeness. Lumenary should clearly direct acute crisis toward human emergency and clinical support.
title: Suicide Facts

notes: Gallup workplace report on employee engagement, stress, loneliness, and the quality of work life. Use as grounding for modern work as a source of identity, exhaustion, isolation, and the need for practices that distinguish responsibility from self-erasure.
title: State of the Global Workplace 2024

notes: World Happiness Report edition with attention to age, wellbeing, social support, and declines among younger people in several countries. Use as grounding for generational distress, belonging, loneliness, future anxiety, and the need to write teachings for the modern person.
title: World Happiness Report 2024
